title: भीषण गर्मी से राहत देगा 'ह्यूमन रेफ्रिजरेटर', जापान की कंपनी ने बनाया खास बूथ - NewsBytes
search_term: (लू OR "लू चलना" OR "भीषण गर्मी" OR "प्रचंड गर्मी" OR "कड़ी धूप" OR "तेज धूप" OR "गर्म हवा" OR "उष्ण लहर" OR "ताप लहर" OR "हीट वेव" OR "झुलसाने वाली गर्मी" OR "तपती गर्मी" OR "भयंकर गर्मी" OR "गर्मी का कहर" OR "गर्मी का प्रकोप" OR "अत्यधिक गर्मी" OR मौसम OR "मौसम अपडेट" OR "मौसम का हाल" OR "मौसम पूर्वानुमान" OR "मौसम अलर्ट" OR आईएमडी OR "आईएमडी पूर्वानुमान" OR "आईएमडी अलर्ट" OR "तापमान में बढ़ोतरी" OR "गर्मी से राहत" OR "गर्मी से निजात") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
भीषण गर्मी से राहत देगा 'ह्यूमन रेफ्रिजरेटर', जापान की कंपनी ने बनाया खास बूथ
क्या है खबर?
दुनियाभर में बढ़ती गर्मी और लगातार बढ़ते तापमान के बीच जापान की एक कंपनी ने लोगों को राहत देने के लिए खास "ह्यूमन रेफ्रिजरेटर" तैयार किया है। इसका नाम डो हीमोन बॉक्स है। यह दिखने में रेफ्रिजरेटर जैसा है और इसके अंदर बैठकर व्यक्ति अपने शरीर को तेजी से ठंडा कर सकता है। इस बूथ को जापानी रेफ्रिजरेशन और वेंडिंग मशीन बनाने वाली कंपनी SDRS ने विकसित किया है, जिसका उद्देश्य भीषण गर्मी से लोगों को तुरंत राहत पहुंचाना है।
खासियत
कुछ मिनटों में शरीर को करता है ठंडा
इस बूथ के अंदर एक कुर्सी है, जहां व्यक्ति आराम से बैठ सकता है।
बूथ का तापमान करीब 15 डिग्री सेल्सियस रखा जाता है। जैसे ही कोई व्यक्ति बैठता है, 5 डिग्री सेल्सियस ठंडी हवा सिर, गर्दन, कंधों और पीठ की ओर पहुंचने लगती है।
कंपनी का दावा है कि करीब पांच मिनट में शरीर को ठंडक महसूस होने लगती है और दस मिनट में तापमान काफी कम हो सकता है, जिससे गर्मी से होने वाली परेशानी घट सकती है।
काम
कम बिजली में करता है बेहतर काम
कंपनी के अनुसार, यह ह्यूमन रेफ्रिजरेटर सामान्य स्पॉट एयर कंडीशनर की तुलना में आधी बिजली खर्च करता है।
इसमें हवा और ठंडक के तीन अलग-अलग विकल्प दिए गए हैं। साथ ही यह 20 मिनट बाद अपने आप बंद हो जाता है, ताकि शरीर जरूरत से ज्यादा ठंडा न हो।
इसमें पहिए भी लगे हैं, जिससे इसे आसानी से एक जगह से दूसरी जगह ले जाया जा सकता है और जरूरत पड़ने पर तुरंत किसी स्थान पर पहुंचाया जा सकता है।
मांग
जापान में बढ़ रही इसकी मांग
अप्रैल में बिक्री शुरू होने के बाद से इस ह्यूमन रेफ्रिजरेटर की मांग तेजी से बढ़ी है।
इसे कई सार्वजनिक स्थानों पर लगाया जा चुका है, जहां लोग इसका इस्तेमाल कर रहे हैं। फिलहाल इसकी कीमत करीब 15 लाख येन (लगभग 9 लाख रुपये) रखी गई है।
अभी यह उत्पाद केवल कंपनियों और संस्थानों के लिए उपलब्ध है और आम लोगों की बिक्री के लिए नहीं है, हालांकि भविष्य में इसका दायरा और बढ़ने की उम्मीद की जा रही है।

title: IIT रुड़की व FRI का बड़ा खुलासा: पश्चिमी हिमालय में 1°C तापमान बढ़ने से होंगी 112 अतिरिक्त जंगल में आग की घटनाएं - iit roorkee and fri study reveals one degree celsius rise in western himalayan temperature could trigger o - Jagran
search_term: ("पारा चढ़ा" OR "पारा पहुंचा" OR तापमान OR "तापमान बढ़ा" OR "रिकॉर्ड तोड़ गर्मी" OR "डिग्री सेल्सियस" OR "पारा उबला" OR "अधिकतम तापमान" OR "तापमान 40 पार" OR "तापमान 45 पार" OR "तापमान बढ़ने की संभावना") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
IIT रुड़की व FRI का बड़ा खुलासा: पश्चिमी हिमालय में 1°C तापमान बढ़ने से होंगी 112 अतिरिक्त जंगल में आग की घटनाएं
शोध में पाया गया कि पश्चिमी हिमालय में वार्षिक तापमान में एक डिग्री सेल्सियस की वृद्धि से 112 अतिरिक्त वनाग्नि की घटनाएं हो सकती हैं। ...और पढ़ें
HighLights
एक डिग्री तापमान वृद्धि से 112 अतिरिक्त वनाग्नि घटनाएं
आईआईटी रुड़की और वन अनुसंधान संस्थान का संयुक्त शोध
मानवीय गतिविधियां और सूखा मौसम आग के मुख्य कारण
जागरण संवाददाता, देहरादून। जलवायु परिवर्तन अब केवल भविष्य की आशंका नहीं, बल्कि पश्चिमी हिमालय के जंगलों में दिखाई देने वाली वास्तविकता बन चुका है।
भारतीय प्रौद्योगिकी संस्थान (आइआइटी) रुड़की और वन अनुसंधान संस्थान, देहरादून के विज्ञानियों की ओर से किए गए शोध में पाया गया कि औसत वार्षिक तापमान में हर एक डिग्री सेल्सियस वृद्धि के साथ पश्चिमी हिमालय में औसतन 112 अतिरिक्त वनाग्नि की घटनाएं हो सकती हैं।
यदि केवल गर्मियों के औसत तापमान में एक डिग्री सेल्सियस की वृद्धि होती है, तो यह संख्या बढ़कर 128 अतिरिक्त घटनाओं तक पहुंच सकती है।
यह अध्ययन प्रतिष्ठित अंतरराष्ट्रीय जर्नल ट्रीज, फारेस्ट एंड पीपुल में प्रकाशित किया गया है। आइआइटी रुड़की के विज्ञानी अखिल सिंह चरक के नेतृत्व में विज्ञानियों ने वर्ष 2013 से 2022 के बीच पश्चिमी हिमालय में दर्ज 18,645 वनाग्नि की घटनाओं का विश्लेषण किया।
अध्ययन का क्षेत्र उत्तराखंड, हिमाचल प्रदेश, जम्मू-कश्मीर और लद्दाख तक फैला था। उद्देश्य यह जानना था कि आखिर किन कारणों से जंगलों में आग लगती है और कौन से कारक इस खतरे को सबसे अधिक बढ़ाते हैं।
39 कारकों की जांच, 13 सबसे महत्वपूर्ण निकले
शोध में कुल 39 विभिन्न कारकों का परीक्षण किया गया। इनमें जलवायु, भौगोलिक स्थिति, वनस्पति, मिट्टी और मानव गतिविधियों से जुड़े संकेतक शामिल थे।
इनमें से 13 जैव-जलवायु (बायोक्लाइमेटिक) कारक वनाग्नि की घटनाओं को प्रभावित करने वाले सबसे महत्वपूर्ण कारक पाए गए। विज्ञानियों के अनुसार, बढ़ता तापमान जंगल की नमी को तेजी से कम करता है।
जब पत्तियां, घास, चीड़ की सूखी सुइयां और अन्य जैविक पदार्थ सूख जाते हैं, तो वे मामूली चिंगारी से भी आग पकड़ लेते हैं। गर्म मौसम में मिट्टी और वनस्पति दोनों तेजी से सूखते हैं, जिससे आग लगने और फैलने की आशंका कई गुना बढ़ जाती है।
मानव गतिविधियों की भूमिका भी बड़ी
शोध के अनुसार जिन क्षेत्रों में सड़कें, बस्तियां और अन्य मानव गतिविधियां अधिक हैं, वहां वनाग्नि का जोखिम भी अधिक पाया गया। इसका कारण यह है कि अधिकांश आग प्राकृतिक कारणों से नहीं, बल्कि मानवीय लापरवाही, कृषि अवशेष जलाने, कैंप फायर, सिगरेट या अन्य गतिविधियों से शुरू होती है।
खबरें और भी
बढ़ा हुआ तापमान इन छोटी घटनाओं को बड़ी वनाग्नि में बदल देता है। अध्ययन में बताया गया है कि प्री-मानसून (मार्च से जून) का समय सबसे संवेदनशील होता है।
इस अवधि में सर्दियों के बाद जंगलों में बड़ी मात्रा में सूखी जैविक सामग्री जमा रहती है, जबकि तापमान बढ़ जाता है और आर्द्रता घट जाती है। यदि इस दौरान वर्षा और हिमपात सामान्य से कम हो, तो वनाग्नि का खतरा और बढ़ जाता है।

title: जापान में पहली बार दर्ज हुआ ‘कोकुशोबी’ दिन, तीन शहरों का तापमान 40 डिग्री सेल्सियस के पार - bhaskarhindi.com
search_term: ("पारा चढ़ा" OR "पारा पहुंचा" OR तापमान OR "तापमान बढ़ा" OR "रिकॉर्ड तोड़ गर्मी" OR "डिग्री सेल्सियस" OR "पारा उबला" OR "अधिकतम तापमान" OR "तापमान 40 पार" OR "तापमान 45 पार" OR "तापमान बढ़ने की संभावना") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
जापान में पहली बार दर्ज हुआ ‘कोकुशोबी’ दिन, तीन शहरों का तापमान 40 डिग्री सेल्सियस के पार
By - |21 July 2026 3:20 PM IST
जापान में मंगलवार को भीषण गर्मी का नया रिकॉर्ड दर्ज किया गया। देश के मध्य हिस्से के तीन शहरों में तापमान 40 डिग्री सेल्सियस तक पहुंच गया, जिसके साथ ही जापान मौसम विज्ञान एजेंसी (जेएमए) की ओर से इस साल की शुरुआत में पेश किए गए नए शब्द "कोकुशोबी" के तहत देश का पहला "अत्यंत गर्म दिन" आधिकारिक रूप से दर्ज हुआ।
टोक्यो, 21 जुलाई (आईएएनएस)। जापान में मंगलवार को भीषण गर्मी का नया रिकॉर्ड दर्ज किया गया। देश के मध्य हिस्से के तीन शहरों में तापमान 40 डिग्री सेल्सियस तक पहुंच गया, जिसके साथ ही जापान मौसम विज्ञान एजेंसी (जेएमए) की ओर से इस साल की शुरुआत में पेश किए गए नए शब्द "कोकुशोबी" के तहत देश का पहला "अत्यंत गर्म दिन" आधिकारिक रूप से दर्ज हुआ।
क्योडो न्यूज एजेंसी ने जापान मौसम विज्ञान एजेंसी के हवाले से बताया कि एक हाई-प्रेशर सिस्टम के कारण पूरे जापानी द्वीपसमूह के बड़े हिस्से में गर्म हवा का प्रभाव बढ़ गया। इसके चलते गिफू प्रांत के ताजिमी शहर में तापमान 40.3 डिग्री सेल्सियस, गुजो शहर में 40.0 डिग्री सेल्सियस और आइची प्रांत के टोयोदा शहर में 40.1 डिग्री सेल्सियस दर्ज किया गया।
मौसम एजेंसी ने अप्रैल में "कोकुशोबी" शब्द को शामिल किया था। इसका इस्तेमाल उन दिनों के लिए किया जाता है जब तापमान 40 डिग्री सेल्सियस या उससे अधिक पहुंच जाता है। इसका उद्देश्य लोगों को अत्यधिक गर्मी के खतरों के प्रति पहले से अधिक प्रभावी तरीके से सचेत करना है।
मौसम विभाग ने चेतावनी दी है कि बुधवार से देश के कई हिस्सों में तापमान ऊंचा बना रह सकता है। एजेंसी ने लोगों से हीट स्ट्रोक और गर्मी से जुड़ी अन्य बीमारियों से बचने के लिए सावधानी बरतने की अपील की है।
जापान के कुल 914 मौसम निगरानी केंद्रों में से मंगलवार दोपहर 3 बजे तक 731 केंद्रों पर अधिकतम तापमान 30 डिग्री सेल्सियस या उससे अधिक दर्ज किया गया। इनमें से 272 स्थानों पर तापमान इतना अधिक रहा कि उन्हें "मोशोबी ", यानी "अत्यधिक गर्म दिन" की श्रेणी में रखा गया। हालांकि, अलग से वर्गीकृत किए गए "कोकुशोबी" दिनों को इसमें शामिल नहीं किया गया।
मौसम एजेंसी के अनुसार, रविवार के आसपास से उच्च दबाव प्रणाली ने जापान के बड़े हिस्से को प्रभावित करना शुरू किया। इसके कारण जमीन के पास और ऊपरी वायुमंडल दोनों में गर्मी बढ़ी, जिससे तापमान में तेज वृद्धि हुई।
अन्य कई क्षेत्रों में भी तापमान 40 डिग्री के करीब पहुंच गया। नागोया शहर में तापमान 39.7 डिग्री सेल्सियस और सैतामा प्रांत के कुमागाया शहर में 38.6 डिग्री सेल्सियस दर्ज किया गया।

title: Extreme heat is eroding health, productivity and incomes of Dhaka’s informal workers - news - Mongabay
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
- Nearly 75% of migrants in Dhaka, the capital of Bangladesh, reported being negatively affected by rising temperatures, while 100% reported being affected by extreme weather events.
- Bangladesh’s maximum temperature has increased by 1.1°C since 1980, while the country’s “feels-like” temperature has risen by 4.5°C.
- In 2024 alone, heat-related illnesses and productivity losses contributed to the loss of 250 million workdays, costing Bangladesh an estimated $1.78 billion, equivalent to 0.4% of GDP.
- Bangladesh’s informal economy employs approximately 85% of the national workforce, nearly 60 million people making heat stress not only a public health issue but also a major economic concern.
DHAKA — On June 28, as the afternoon sun beat down on Bangladesh capital Dhaka’s sprawling Tejgaon neighborhood, Samiha Begum gathered her two school-aged children and headed for the shade of Hatirjheel Lake. Their narrow one-room home in a nearby slum had become unbearable.
She said, “The room starts heating up as soon as the sun rises. Even if it rains in the morning, the heat becomes like a boiler once the sun comes out. My children cannot stay inside after returning from school. Thus, I come to the lakeside and take the trees’ shade. Here, the air is cooler than in my house.”
Like Samiha Begum, for millions of informal workers across Bangladesh’s capital, extreme heat is no longer merely a seasonal discomfort. It is becoming a growing threat to health, livelihoods and survival. Samiha Begum works as a cook in a family home near her slum.
A new study by People’s Courage International (PCI) on informal workers in South and Southeast Asia found that 74% of surveyed migrants in Dhaka reported being negatively affected by increasing temperatures, making heat one of the city’s most significant climate threats. The study also found that 100% of surveyed migrants in Dhaka reported being affected by extreme weather events, highlighting the growing vulnerability of low-income urban residents.
Researchers analyzed the reality of informal workers in five cities: Dhaka, Delhi, Jakarta, Kathmandu and Quezon City and “how the precarity of their lives and livelihoods is being dangerously intensified due to heat.” Dhaka recorded the largest productivity losses, reflecting its labor intensive economy, high levels of outdoor and factory work, and limited access to cooling.
The researchers said rising temperatures, increasingly warm nights, overcrowded housing, and unsafe workplaces are trapping workers in a cycle of declining productivity, worsening health and growing financial stress.
According to the United Nations World Urbanization Prospects 2025 report, Dhaka has become the world’s second largest city with 37 million people, while Indonesia’s capital Jakarta ranks top with a population of 41.9 million.
The showed that Dhaka’s heat crisis is particularly acute in the city’s sprawling informal settlements, home to an estimated 4 to 5 million people. Many residents live in cramped, tin-roofed rooms with poor ventilation, limited access to water and sanitation. As temperatures rise, these settlements act like heat traps, leaving informal workers exposed to extreme heat both at work and at home.
A city growing hotter
Bangladesh is among the countries most exposed to rising temperatures. According to a 2025 World Bank report, Bangladesh’s maximum temperature has increased by about 1.1° Celsius (1.9° Fahrenheit) since 1980, while the country’s “feels-like” temperature has risen by 4.5° C (8.1° F), significantly increasing heat stress on people and communities.
The report found that Bangladesh ranks among the world’s most heat-exposed countries, while Dhaka’s heat index has increased approximately 65% higher than the national average. In 2024 alone, heat-related physical and mental health impacts contributed to the loss of an estimated 250 million workdays, costing the economy as much as $1.78 billion, equivalent to about 0.4% of gross domestic product.
Bangladesh Meteorological Department’s Meteorologist S.M. Quamrul Hassan told Mongabay that Bangladesh’s average temperatures have increased noticeably over the past decade.
“In the past, severe heat was mainly a summer phenomenon. Now, we are experiencing extreme heat even during the monsoon season. The rainfall patterns are changing. Although total rainfall has not declined significantly, rain is increasingly falling over shorter periods,” he said.
“Earlier, rainfall could continue for six to 12 hours or even throughout the day. Now, intense rainfall often ends within two or three hours. After that, strong sunshine quickly drives temperatures upward.”
Heat and lost livelihoods
The consequences are both economic and physical. The PCI study found that among surveyed migrants in Dhaka, major work-related impacts included wage cuts, wage losses, and loss of employment-related assets. Workers reported that heat reduces their ability to sustain long hours of physical labor, directly affecting earnings.
Tayeb Ali, a rickshaw puller living in Rayerbazar slum in Dhaka, said he now needs frequent breaks. “Ten minutes of running rickshaw means twenty minutes of rest. I get exhausted quickly,” he said, of manually operating the passenger transport cart.
His family of four shares a cramped room where several electric fans run continuously.
“Even with extra fans, we cannot escape the heat,” Tayeb Ali said. “At night, I cannot sleep properly. In the morning, I wake up late, which means fewer working hours and less income.”
Researchers describe this phenomenon as a “recovery deficit.” Workers spend long hours exposed to heat during the day but are unable to recover at night, because their homes remain dangerously hot. Rising nighttime temperatures are increasingly depriving vulnerable workers of essential rest and recovery.
The health toll of invisible heat
The impacts extend far beyond lost wages. The migrant survey found that among Dhaka respondents, 71% reported health problems linked to extreme weather, while 69% reported food insecurity and 66% reported difficulties accessing water and sanitation services.
Global health research shows that prolonged exposure to heat can lead to dehydration, exhaustion, heat stroke, cardiovascular stress, respiratory illness and kidney disease. Informal workers are particularly vulnerable, because many work outdoors or in poorly ventilated environments.
Different workers who live in Dhaka’s informal settlements said that they face headaches, dizziness, fatigue, fainting and worsening respiratory conditions during periods of extreme heat. Many continue working despite symptoms because missing work means losing income.
Mirpur area resident Hamida Begum says her children now fall sick more frequently during hot periods. “They sweat all day and become ill more often than before,” she said.
A recent study documented 35 heat-wave events in Dhaka between 2001 and 2022, with an increasing trend in both frequency and duration. The study also highlighted growing urban heat island hotspots across the city.
Bangladesh Bureau of Statistics (BBS) data show that Bangladesh’s informal economy employs approximately 85% of the national workforce, representing nearly 60 million workers.

full_text:
Sweltering heat sparked surge in insects in North Holland
Sweltering summer heat has accelerated insect reproduction across Noord-Holland, prompting a sharp rise in pest control calls as colonies of ants, moths, flies and other insects expand rapidly, experts told NH.
Temperatures above 25 degrees Celsius speed up development from egg to larva to adult for most insect species, allowing populations to boom in a short time, according to Gerhard Geurtse from the Kennis- en Adviescentrum Dierplagen, or KAD.
The KAD has received many more inquiries this summer from residents battling infestations. Pest control companies are also reporting a noticeable increase in demand.
"As soon as it gets warm, reproduction goes a lot faster. Then it really explodes," Richard Piké from Beestjes Kwijt pest control told NH.
Ants have created the most immediate headaches. Warm conditions have helped colonies grow quickly, sending the insects indoors in large numbers in search of food. A single crumb or sweet drink is often enough to draw long trails of ants into kitchens in areas including Haarlemmermeer, the Gooi region, IJmond, Noordkop & Texel, Schiphol, West-Friesland, and Zaanstreek-Waterland.
Moths are also surging. Food moths, known as pantry pests, lay eggs in long-shelf-life products such as rice, flour, macaroni, and nuts. Heat makes the eggs hatch faster, larvae develop more quickly, and adults spread throughout homes to lay more eggs.
Clothing moths present a separate problem. Their larvae feed on wool, carpets, fur, and other fabrics, chewing holes in clothing and other items. Adult moths do not cause damage.
Experts advise storing dry food products in airtight jars or containers. If larvae or webbing appears, the packaging should be discarded immediately.
Flies, especially meat flies and carrion flies, are thriving in the high temperatures, Geurtse told NH. They congregate around food scraps, trash bins, and compost piles.
Mosquitoes also develop rapidly in warm conditions but need water to breed. Rain or leftover water in rain barrels and buckets can lead to quick population growth.
Wasp activity reportedly remains relatively calm for now. Their nests are still developing, Geurtse said, and significant problems typically arise later in summer when colonies grow larger and the insects become more aggressive.

title: WorkSafeBC warns employers to protect workers from heat stress, heat stroke - Surrey Now-Leader

full_text:
WorkSafeBC warns employers to protect workers from heat stress, heat stroke
Published 10:30 am Tuesday, July 21, 2026
WorkSafeBC has issued a bulletin to remind employers that they must protect their workers from heat-related illnesses as Environment Canada issues heat alerts.
This applies to staffers working inside and outside.
Todd McDonald, WorkSafeBC’s head of prevention services, warned that higher temperatures by nature increase the risk of heat stress and heat stroke, “which can be serious and even life-threatening.
“These illnesses are preventable, and employers have a responsibility to take steps to protect workers, whether they are indoors or outdoors,” McDonald said.
WorkSafeBC received 335 heat-related injury claims between 2021 and 2025, with 47 of them in 2025.
The July 21 bulletin notes those working in construction, agriculture, transportation, and public works are at higher risk but people working indoors — such as in kitchens, warehouses, and manufacturing buildings — are also susceptible, particularly if there’s no air conditioning.
WorkSafeBC requires employers to conduct risk assessments and develop safety plans.
Accordingly, they should provide cool drinking water and regular rest breaks, set up shady or cooled recovery areas, schedule strenuous task during cooler times in the day, improve ventilation, and rotate workers between hotter and cooler environments.
Employers also need to train their workers to recognize early signs of heat stress and how to access first aid. The bulletin notes that symptoms can include excessive sweating, muscle cramps, nausea, weakness, dizziness, agitation and confusion.
Workers should report symptoms and be encouraged to speak up if things are not safe.
Employees during hot weather should also drink lots of water – a glass every 20 minutes – wear sunscreen, wear light breathable clothing like cotton, be aware of their personal risks related to medications or health conditions, and watch for signs of heat stress in themselves and co-workers.
Anyone with questions about heat stress can call 604-276-3100 or toll-free at 1-888-621-7233 for information.

title: Heatstroke guide assessment app advises on medical response - 朝日新聞
search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
By KAZUYA GOTO/ Staff Writer
July 21, 2026 at 18:33 JST
An ambulance responds to an emergency call. (Asahi Shimbun file photo)
As sweltering temperatures sweep across Japan and increasing heat-related illnesses are reported, a new app has been released to assess the severity of heatstroke and allow emergency authorities to monitor trends.
A team from Nippon Medical School and the National Institute for Environmental Studies developed the app that allows people experiencing symptoms, as well as their family members or those nearby, to evaluate the severity by answering a series of simple questions.
The app also provides guidance on appropriate responses based on the severity level, including whether an ambulance should be called, and adds to a database that officials can monitor.
“We would like as many people as possible to learn about this initiative,” said Shoji Yokobori, a professor at Nippon Medical School, at a news conference. “Because it enables heat-related illness cases to be tracked in real time, it may allow local authorities to implement countermeasures more quickly, adjust the number of ambulances in service and improve the capacity of medical institutions to accept patients.”
The number of people transported by ambulance for heat-related illnesses nationwide in 2025 exceeded 100,000 for the first time.
Until now, there has been no system for monitoring cases of heat-related illnesses as they occur, and the risk of the illnesses has been indicated through warning levels based on the wet-bulb globe temperature (WBGT) heat stress index.
In the newly developed app, the information entered by users is stored in a database together with location data. As a result, administrative authorities, fire departments and medical institutions can monitor occurrences of heat-related illnesses in real time.
An analysis of data from heat-related illness patients collected through an app previously developed by the Japanese Association for Acute Medicine found that 5 percent of patients suffered physical impairment, with headaches being the most common symptom.
The study also indicated that people who have experienced such illnesses in the past should exercise caution.
In the new app, users enter information such as pre-existing medical conditions, age and symptoms. The app then assesses the risk level of heat-related illnesses on a four-tier scale based on guidelines from the Japanese Association for Acute Medicine.
It also provides recommendations on how to respond, such as continue monitoring the situation, seek medical attention or call an ambulance.
Fukuoka Prefecture will be the first to introduce the app by notifying residents, municipal fire departments and other relevant organizations about its use.
Administrative authorities, fire departments and medical institutions in the prefecture will utilize the data to allow early intervention.
The developers also plan to invite local governments across Japan to participate and gradually expand the data sharing.
The new app is available at the following link. (https://heatstroke.app/)

Japanese weather officials are warning that temperatures could soar to dangerous levels in some areas on Tuesday, urging caution against heatstroke.
Scorching heat gripped wide areas of Japan from the Tohoku region in the northeast to Kyushu in the southwest on Monday. The mercury soared to extreme levels mainly in inland areas. A daytime high of 39.4 degrees Celsius was recorded in Koshu City, Yamanashi Prefecture.
The officials expect temperatures to rise again on Tuesday to similar levels over expansive parts of the country. They forecast daytime highs could reach 40 degrees in Kumagaya City, Saitama Prefecture, 39 degrees in the cities of Nagoya and Maebashi, and 38 degrees in the cities of Saitama and Kofu.
Heatstroke alerts have been issued for 42 of the country's 47 prefectures, from the Tohoku region to Okinawa.
People are advised to use air-conditioners and consume adequate amounts of water and salt to prevent heatstroke. They are also urged to avoid long outdoor activities.

title: SpaceX Is Down 45% From Its All-Time High, and the Pain Is Just Beginning for Shareholders - The Motley Fool
search_term: ("mercury soars" OR "mercury crosses" OR "temperature soars" OR "record temperature" OR "all-time high" OR "degrees Celsius" OR "maximum temperature" OR "hottest day" OR "temperature crosses 40" OR "temperature crosses 45" OR "temperature likely to rise") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
Six weeks ago, Elon Musk's artificial intelligence (AI) and space infrastructure conglomerate, Space Exploration Technologies (SpaceX) (SPCX +3.19%), was the talk of Wall Street.
On June 12, SpaceX raised $85.7 billion from its initial public offering (IPO), including the underwriters' overallotment. This nearly tripled the previous largest-ever IPO capital raise of $29.4 billion from overseas oil giant Saudi Aramco.
But IPO buzz fades quickly on Wall Street, and reality can hit even the most-hyped stocks like a ton of bricks. Since peaking at $225.64 per share intra-day on June 16, SpaceX stock has plunged 45% to less than $124 per share (as of the July 17 close).
Some investors will undoubtedly see a bargain, given Elon Musk's track record at Tesla and SpaceX's opportunity amid the two hottest trends on Wall Street: AI and the space economy. I see far more pain to come for shareholders as historical precedent takes hold.
The accelerated unlock period is quickly approaching
For starters, SpaceX's insiders (high-ranking executives, board members, and early investors) are set to enjoy the greatest wealth transfer in history. In a matter of weeks, most insiders will be able to sell a portion of their shares to retail investors.
Whereas most newly public companies adhere to a 180-day lockup period, in which insiders can't sell their shares, SpaceX offers a staggered and accelerated unlock schedule that begins two days after the company's first quarterly operating report as a public company. SpaceX is currently estimated to report its latest quarterly operating results on Aug. 6.
Great look at the SpaceX shares unlock schedule as well as the potential passive buying schedule from @JSeyff @FrancisSharoon Depending on the early post-IPO returns, this could really play with and disperse the returns of "passive" funds (which is why there's arguably no such... pic.twitter.com/KOuEkJlngF
-- Eric Balchunas (@EricBalchunas) May 28, 2026
The company's float is set to grow every few weeks through mid-December, adding downside pressure on SpaceX stock.
Historically, SpaceX's valuation is a nightmare
Although it's not uncommon for investors to place high premiums on companies at the forefront of game-changing technologies, SpaceX's valuation is historical nightmare fuel.
No company heralding the charge of a leading innovation has ever sustained a price-to-sales ratio above 30 for any lengthy period. SpaceX is currently trading at 42 times Wall Street's consensus sales estimate for this year. In other words, Musk's company would need to fall nearly 30% more from its current level just to push below historic bubble territory.
Furthermore, the company isn't particularly close to recurring profits, and its capital-intensive operating model leaves virtually no margin for error or delays.
Debt and equity offerings are coming
To round things out, SpaceX's prospectus made clear that, in addition to its IPO capital raise, debt and equity offerings would be used to fund the company's AI infrastructure expansion, among other corporate initiatives.
Less than two weeks after going public, the company priced a $25 billion bond offering, with maturities from 2031 to 2056. The price of these bonds has been falling steadily since issuance, signifying concern from bondholders that SpaceX may be unable to meet its obligations.
Additionally, equity offerings would be dilutive to existing shareholders. Given that SpaceX is spending a small fortune on its AI data center build-out, capital-raising activity that weighs on the company's shares is a near-certainty.

full_text:
As Delhi and the National Capital Region (NCR) welcome rainfall, which brought respite from prolonged humid weather, the Indian Meteorological Department (IMD) has forecast intermittent showers for the next two days.
The weather agency has also predicted isolated heavy rainfall across northern states, including Haryana, Punjab, Chandigarh, and Delhi - NCR till July 27.
Thunderstorms accompanied by strong winds of 40-50 kmph are also expected in parts of Andhra Pradesh, Yanam, and Telangana. The southern states, along with Karnataka, and western ghat regions of Tamil Nadu are expected to receive moderate rainfall between July 25 and July 27.
A red alert has been issued for Himachal Pradesh and Jammu & Kashmir. According to government reports, 24 people lost their lives in the past two days due to landslides in Jammu & Kashmir, and six remain missing. Rescue and relief operations are currently ongoing. Rainfall is expected to exceed 204.5 mm in isolated locations over the two states. Wind speeds reaching 30-40 kmph are expected to continue until July 27.
The IMD has also issued a Red alert for several districts of Uttarakhand, while the rest of the state has been issued with an orange alert. Continuous rainfall in the state has triggered landslides and floods, which have led to blocked roads. Local administrations have advised residents to avoid rivers and streams due to the increased river level.
Around 98 roads have been closed in the Himalayan region, disrupting connectivity, which has affected daily life across several towns, villages, and remote areas. The authorities have temporarily suspended the Char Dham Yatra, Amarnath Yatra, Mata Vaishno Devi pilgrimage, and pilgrimages to Shiv Khori in Reasi and Sarthal in Kishtwar, as a precautionary measure due to the increased risk of landslides along the pilgrimage routes.
Heavy to very heavy rainfall ranging between 115.6 mm and 204.4 mm is expected across the northern parts of the country, including in Assam, Meghalaya, Arunachal Pradesh, Nagaland, Manipur, Mizoram, Tripura, and Sikkim. Heavy rainfall is also forecast for Bihar, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, Odisha, Madhya Pradesh, Uttar Pradesh, Rajasthan, Gujarat, West Bengal, Goa, Maharashtra, and the Konkan region.

title: Yellow alert for Nashik as showers boost dam stocks, farm activity - The Times of India
search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
Nashik: The
India Meteorological Department has issued a yellow alert for Nashik district for Wednesday and Thursday, forecasting heavy rainfall at isolated places and indicating that the current monsoon spell is likely to continue across the region.
The recent showers have improved water availability and accelerated agricultural activity, particularly in the district’s tribal belt. According to the rainfall data recorded in the 24 hours ending 8.30am on Tuesday, Surgana and Trimbakeshwar talukas received more than 55mm rainfall each, while Peth recorded 27mm.
Agriculture officials said the fresh monsoon spell has significantly boosted paddy transplantation in key rice-growing areas over the past two weeks. Improved soil moisture has also strengthened prospects for the kharif season after initial concerns over delayed monsoon progress.
The rainfall in the catchment areas has led to a sharp rise in storage at the Gangapur dam, Nashik city’s main drinking water source. Officials said water stock in the reservoir has reached 74.3% of its capacity following continuous inflows from upstream areas.
Gangapur dam meets nearly 80% of Nashik city’s drinking water needs.
The improved storage has eased concerns over water availability that had surfaced earlier due to delayed monsoon rainfall.
However, Nashik Municipal Corporation (NMC) has decided to continue the once-a-week water cut introduced on June 20. Civic officials said the Saturday shutdown will remain in force until sustained rainfall further improves water reserves and ensures adequate storage for the coming months.
Weather officials have advised residents, especially those in low-lying and vulnerable areas, to remain cautious as intermittent heavy showers are likely over the next few days. Nashik city has been receiving light to moderate rainfall since Monday.
Meanwhile, persistent rainfall has worsened road conditions across the city, exposing potholes and damaged stretches at several locations. Since asphalt repairs are not feasible during active monsoon conditions, NMC is carrying out temporary pothole-filling using granular sub-base (GSB) material.
Municipal officials said permanent resurfacing and road repair works would be undertaken after the monsoon, when weather conditions become conducive for durable restoration. Road damage has emerged as a major civic issue in recent weeks, drawing repeated complaints from residents and public representatives.

The weather office has placed Delhi under a yellow alert for Tuesday and Wednesday. It expects several spells of light to moderate rain, accompanied by thunderstorms and strong winds at most places until noon, while isolated areas may receive heavy rainfall. Her work ranges from fast-paced breaking updates to sensitive human-interest features, blending speed with clarity.

With over four years of experience in news and trend reporting, Kanishka has worked with leading organisations such as Hindustan Times and Times Now. She moves seamlessly between profiling business leaders and telling the stories of everyday people, covering national developments just as effortlessly as the memes and conversations that dominate online culture.

She also reports on real estate developments and civic challenges in major urban hubs like Bengaluru, Delhi, and Gurugram. Her coverage frequently explores the struggles of startup founders, inspiring journeys of CEOs, and the experiences of candidates dealing with the complexities of visa processes.

Kanishka holds an undergraduate degree in Journalism from Delhi University and a diploma from the Indian Institute of Mass Communication. Rarely offline, she spends much of her time scrolling through X, LinkedIn, Reddit, Instagram, and Facebook in search of the next big story. When she finally logs off, she enjoys binge-watching shows and exploring cafes in pursuit of good food and better conversations.

title: IMD issues red alert in Kangra, Chamba, Sirmaur - News Arena India
search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
The weather agency has warned of heavy to very heavy rain, with isolated spells of extremely heavy rainfall, as the southwest monsoon remains active over the state.
News Arena Network - Shimla - UPDATED: July 21, 2026, 05:13 PM - 2 min read
Visual from the site.
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued a red alert for Kangra, Chamba and Sirmaur districts in Himachal Pradesh. The weather agency has warned of heavy to very heavy rain, with isolated spells of extremely heavy rainfall, as the southwest monsoon remains active over the state.
IMD Himachal Pradesh Senior Scientist Sandeep Kumar Sharma said in Shimla on Tuesday that most parts of the state received rain over the past 24 hours. Kangra, Chamba and Sirmaur recorded the heaviest showers during this period.
“Over the past 24 hours, widespread rainfall has been recorded across Himachal Pradesh. Kangra, Chamba and Sirmaur witnessed heavy to very heavy rainfall. Since late Monday night, weather conditions have remained particularly vigorous over Kangra and Chamba. Palampur recorded the highest rainfall of 140 mm till 8:30 a.m., and rainfall has continued there even after that. In Sirmaur, Rajgarh, Paonta Sahib and Nahan experienced heavy to very heavy rainfall, with Rajgarh recording around 105 mm,” Sharma said.
He said heavy showers were also reported from Churah and Jot in Chamba district. Rainfall is continuing in several areas of Chamba and Kangra, as well as neighbouring parts of Kullu and Mandi.
He said the weather is likely to remain severe on Tuesday and through the night of July 21-22. Kangra and Chamba are expected to be the worst affected areas.
"Another strong spell of rain is expected in Kangra and Chamba during the night and early morning. A Red Alert has therefore been issued for both districts, warning of heavy to very heavy rain and isolated extremely heavy rainfall. Sirmaur may not receive rain as intense as it did over the last two days, but the continued rainfall has increased the cumulative impact. This is why the Red Alert has been extended there for the next 24 hours," he said.
Rainfall in Kullu and Mandi is expected to be less intense, Sharma said. The IMD has therefore placed these districts under an orange alert. Shimla is also under an orange alert for heavy to very heavy rainfall. The weather is likely to start improving from Wednesday, he said.
"Rainfall activity will gradually weaken from July 22. An orange alert will mainly remain in place for Kangra and Mandi, while Chamba, Shimla and Solan will be under a yellow alert for heavy rain. A major reduction in rainfall is expected from July 23," Sharma said.
He, however, said rain would continue in different parts of Himachal Pradesh till July 23. On July 24 and 25, intermittent showers are likely mainly over the mid-hills and nearby plains.
"Isolated heavy rain may still occur in Solan, Shimla, Sirmaur, Kangra and Una on July 24 and 25," he added. The senior IMD scientist also warned of a possible rise in flash flood risks in several areas because the soil is already saturated after continuous rainfall.
"Heavy to very heavy rain over Shimla, parts of Mandi near Kullu, Kangra and Chamba during the last 24 hours has raised the possibility of flash floods. Any further spell of intense rainfall could lead to flash flood situations. Advisories have therefore been issued for these areas," Sharma said.
Speaking about the rainfall situation in July, Sharma said four districts — Bilaspur, Hamirpur, Lahaul-Spiti and Mandi — have received less rain than normal so far this month. The other eight districts have recorded rainfall above the normal level.
He said Himachal Pradesh has received 14 per cent less rainfall than normal between June 1 and July 21. However, Solan, Una, Sirmaur, Shimla, Kullu, Kinnaur and Chamba have received rainfall close to their seasonal average.
Widespread rain also brought down maximum temperatures across the state by around 2 to 4 degrees Celsius in the last 24 hours. "Maximum temperatures in Shimla, Solan and Dharamshala were between 21 and 23 degrees Celsius. Bilaspur, Hamirpur and Mandi saw the biggest drop of around three to four degrees, with temperatures settling between 26 and 28 degrees Celsius. Paonta Sahib recorded the highest maximum temperature in the state at 32 degrees Celsius," Sharma said.
He said there is unlikely to be much change in daytime temperatures over the next two days. Temperatures are expected to remain below normal in several parts of Himachal Pradesh.
Sharma also warned that low clouds could reduce visibility in Shimla, Solan and Sirmaur. He said the problem would be caused by low cloud cover rather than fog. "Low clouds are likely to cover Shimla, Solan and Sirmaur, leading to poor visibility. Visibility may also fall sharply in areas that receive heavy rainfall," he said.

full_text:
Happy Tuesday, everyone!
A Heat Advisory remains in effect for the Pine Belt through 9 PM tonight.
Make sure to stay hydrated if working outside for an extended time.
Tropical Storm Bertha will have little, if any, impact on our weather in the Pine Belt, but please pay close attention from the First Alert Weather team in case there are changes.
Today, expect sunny and hot weather with highs in the upper 90s and lows in the upper 70s.
The rest of the week appears to be hot and humid with highs in the mid to upper 90s and lows in the mid to upper 70s.
There is a 20 to 30% chance of a thunderstorm on Wednesday into the weekend.
